title: Dog Red Fleshy Mass in Eye Corner: Guide to Cherry Eye
meta_desc: Noticed a red mass in your dog's eye? Learn about Cherry Eye, urgency levels, and how a photo helps your vet with this common eye condition.
content_html:
TL;DR: A red mass in the corner of a dog's eye is typically "Cherry Eye," a prolapsed tear gland that requires a non-emergency veterinary appointment. While usually not painful initially, it needs medical attention to prevent secondary irritation or infection.

What does it mean if my dog has a red fleshy mass in the corner of their eye?

A red, fleshy mass in the inner corner of a dog's eye is most commonly a condition known as 'Cherry Eye.' This occurs when the gland of the third eyelid prolapses, or pops out of its normal position. It typically appears as a smooth, round, pink or red growth near the nose-side of the eye. While it can look quite startling, it is essentially a displaced tear gland.

Is a red mass in my dog's eye considered a medical emergency?

  • The urgency level for a suspected cherry eye is generally low and is not a life-threatening emergency.
  • A scheduled veterinary visit is required, as the exposed gland can become irritated, inflamed, or infected if left untreated.
  • While rarely painful in early stages, you should seek care sooner if your dog is pawing at the eye excessively or if you notice thick, green discharge.

How does providing a photo help my veterinarian triage this eye condition?

  • A clear photo allows a professional to differentiate between a prolapsed gland and more concerning issues like an eyelid tumor, a foreign object, or severe conjunctivitis.
  • By seeing the shape, color, and placement of the mass, your vet can better advise you on how quickly your pet needs to be seen.
  • A visual assessment helps the veterinary team determine the best course of action and what specific symptoms you should monitor.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In young dogs, particularly breeds such as American Cocker Spaniels, Beagles, Lhasa Apsos, Pekingese, and English Bulldogs, hypertrophy, inflammation, and prolapse of the gland of the nictitating membrane (cherry eye) is common. Acutely, a red glandular mass swells and protrudes over the leading margin of the nictitans, often accompanied by mucopurulent discharge. While swelling may temporarily recede, the gland typically remains prolapsed. Given that it is a major tear gland, preservation is crucial, with recommended surgical techniques involving replacement and anchoring of the gland using sutures to the orbital rim, periorbital fascia, or nictitans cartilage, or employing envelope or pocket techniques. Partial excision should be avoided, as complete excision predisposes 30%-40% of dogs to keratoconjunctivitis sicca later in life; even with surgical or medical resolution, approximately 20% of dogs may still develop keratoconjunctivitis sicca, necessitating long-term monitoring.

Chapter: Ophthalmology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 490)

faqs.question: Can I push the mass back in myself?
faqs.answer: It is not recommended to attempt to push the gland back in manually, as you may cause injury to the eye or cause your dog pain. This condition typically requires a surgical 'tuck' by a veterinarian.

faqs.question: Will cherry eye go away on its own?
faqs.answer: No, a prolapsed gland of the third eyelid usually requires medical or surgical intervention to be repositioned permanently.

faqs.question: What happens if I leave it untreated?
faqs.answer: If left untreated, the gland may stop producing tears correctly, which can lead to a chronic condition called Dry Eye (Keratoconjunctivitis Sicca) that requires lifelong medication.
